observations: The opening horse arrival was braided into one longer sentence, giving Jaina's first read a smoother procedural breath instead of three clipped fragments.
The square overview now runs in built sentences rather than a metronomic stack, so the prose sounds like one observing mind instead of a camera ticking from object to object.

Interrogative punctuation was repaired across the captain, infirmary, and granary exchanges: 'What are you calling the sickness?', 'How many?', 'Has she been eating here?', 'Can you fix it?', 'What were you doing in here?', 'What did he look like?', and 'What?'.

Document-colored phrasing in Jaina's grain read was pulled back into story voice: 'conditioning' and 'carrier' became the outer shell singing loudest and the taint being poured in before it touched the grain.
The chapter now closes on carts and a mule-handler's shout instead of abstract personification, keeping the final pressure concrete and road-bound.
Cyndia's reaction to Davan's death now stays visible-only — fingers tightening on the bow until the leather creaks — instead of the narrator assigning her anger a plan.
The captain's turn from denial is rendered as the lie going out of him, which Jaina can read, rather than as a more abstract diagnosis of his internal state.
The opening remains firmly inside Jaina's filter: smell first, magical wrongness second, then the human square as evidence under that read.
Davan's poisoning stays in Jaina's body knowledge — warmth at the throat, foam at the mouth, poison outrunning her wards — without any omniscient explanation of what he experiences.
Sylvanas's competence is shown through methods Jaina has already learned to recognize: silence, pressure, and the way she makes other people finish the thought for her.
The opening still carries chapter3's residue directly: Millhaven's silence is the measure against which this village's ordinary noise fails.
The reduced riding party remains correct throughout chapter4: Jaina, Sylvanas, Cyndia, and Lyriel on the southbound line, with Neral absent after her northbound relay duty.
The chapter preserves the continuity rule that grain is only proved here, not retroactively in Millhaven; chapter3 stays clue, chapter4 becomes confirmation.
Jaina's arc state matches the memory files: she now waits for Sylvanas's human read without softening into easy trust.
The older seller / newer supplier thread stays weak and useful rather than collapsing into premature recognition or named certainty.
The square-arrival paragraph was rebuilt with clause-linking and one participial phrase, breaking the opening telegram run.
Cyndia's horse-handling, Jaina's sickroom movement, the granary recheck, and the closet discovery all gained connective tissue through participial phrasing.
Two semicolon joins were added where the prose wanted carry rather than reset: the Millhaven silence landing on the captain, and Sylvanas letting silence finish her work.

Several fragment stacks were compressed into shaped sentences — 'gut first, then farther,' 'repeated meals, repeated settling, repeated doses,' 'Not one bad granary. Road and storehouse both.' — so the chapter keeps snap without losing flow.

The texture pass raised participial density over the style-guide floor and turned the structural verdict from below-target to within-target, though paragraph cadence remains intentionally brisk because the chapter is dialogue-heavy and investigatory.

continuity.crossChapter: Direct two-day carry preserved from chapter3: the chapter opens measuring living noise against Millhaven's silence, keeps the same four-rider party, and turns that residue into the first on-page proof of an ordinary-looking outbreak.
observations: The opening now leans harder on Millhaven as the immediate emotional comparator, so the chapter break reads as continuation rather than restart.
Jaina's movement from patient patterns to granary certainty stays causal: gut-first clustering, bread correlation, final ward recheck, then the granary door.
The proof does not collapse into one local accident. 'Not one bad granary. Road and storehouse both.' keeps the chapter aligned with the wider distribution threat.
Davan's suicide drives the Andorhal decision cleanly: watcher captured, watcher lost, village triage left behind, source pursued.
The final image of carts and loading pressure points southwest toward Andorhal exactly the way the chapter handoff requires.
Brek's reaction lost the extra explanatory tail 'on principle,' leaving the mistrust to live in the look itself.
The doorway deployment no longer calls itself 'the next beat,' which was planning language showing through the prose.
The broken-vessel moment no longer says 'carrier before mixing'; it now names the physical action instead — what they poured in before it touched the grain.
Jaina's old report-speak fragment 'Supply chain. Deliberate carriage. Hands inside the route.' is now grounded in road, storehouse, and deliberate human movement.
The closing line about morning continuing on principle was replaced with mule, mud, and shouted labor, cutting one more piece of clever abstraction.
The captain's speech stays local and exhausted: fever, bad run, if I still can. Nothing in his mouth sounds bureaucratic.
Brek's healer talk remains field-practical — sweating, gut turns, loose bowels, boiled mint, watered broth — rather than clinical report language.
Jaina's spoken explanations downshift correctly for villagers and soldiers: grain, bread, water, wards, hold till evening. No vector or ingestion jargon survived.
Sylvanas's interrogation lines stay clipped and spoken: 'Ask who sent him.' 'Come with us.' 'Keep breathing.' The authority is in the sentence length, not in institutional vocabulary.
Davan sounds like a frightened village boy all the way through; his speech breaks into scraps and repeated instructions instead of expositional polish.
